content.problemStatement:
For an array BBB consisting of **distinct** integers, its number of inversions is equal to the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) with 1≤i<j≤M1\le i < j \le M1≤i<j≤M such that Bi>BjB_i > B_jBi​>Bj​, where MMM is the size of BBB. If this number is odd, we will call array BBB **odd**. Otherwise, we will call it **even**.

Let's call a permutation PPP of integers from 111 to KKK **good**, if there exists a way to split it into two subsequences, each of which is **odd**.

For example, we can split [5,1,3,2,6,4][5, 1, 3, 2, 6, 4][5,1,3,2,6,4] into subsequences [5,3,2][5, 3, 2][5,3,2] and [1,6,4][1, 6, 4][1,6,4], both of which are odd. There is no such split for the permutation [1,2,4,3,5,6][1, 2, 4, 3, 5, 6][1,2,4,3,5,6].

For each KKK from 111 to NNN, find the number of permutations of integers from 111 to KKK, which are **good**. As this number may be very high, output each number modulo some prime PPP.


Input Format

The only line of the input contains two integers N,PN, PN,P.


Output Format
Print NNN space-separated integers, the KthK^{th}Kth of which is the number of permutations of integers from 111 to KKK which are **good**. Output all values modulo PPP.
